A day on Venus lasts longer than its year. Venus spins so slowly that it circles the Sun before finishing one rotation.
Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest volcano in the solar system, nearly three times the height of Mount Everest.
Saturn is less dense than water. In a big enough bathtub, the planet would float.
Our Sun holds about 99.8 percent of all the mass in the solar system. Everything else is rounding error.
Roughly one million Earths could fit inside the Sun if it were hollow.
Sunlight takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth, so you always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.
Voyager 1 is the most distant human-made object, more than 15 billion miles from Earth and still calling home.
Both Voyager probes carry a golden record with music and greetings, a message in a bottle for any aliens who find it.
The International Space Station circles Earth about every 90 minutes, so its crew sees around 16 sunrises every day.
Astronauts on the space station travel at roughly 17,500 miles per hour, crossing the United States in about 10 minutes.
In weightlessness, astronauts' spines stretch and they can return to Earth up to 2 inches taller, at least for a while.
Footprints left by Apollo astronauts will likely last millions of years, because the Moon has no wind or rain to erase them.
Our Moon is drifting away from Earth at about 3.8 centimeters per year, roughly the speed your fingernails grow.
Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm wider than Earth that has been raging for at least 190 years.
Jupiter has more than 90 known moons, and astronomers keep finding more.
Ganymede, a moon of Jupiter, is bigger than the entire planet Mercury.
Titan, Saturn's largest moon, has rivers, lakes, and rain, but made of liquid methane instead of water.
Beneath its icy shell, Jupiter's moon Europa may hold an ocean with more liquid water than all of Earth's oceans combined.
Some neutron stars spin more than 700 times per second, faster than the blades of a kitchen blender.
A supermassive black hole called Sagittarius A star sits at the center of our galaxy, weighing about 4 million Suns.
There are likely more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on every beach on Earth.
The observable universe stretches about 93 billion light years across, and that is just the part we can see.
Everything in the universe, all of it, began about 13.8 billion years ago in the Big Bang.
Sunsets on Mars are blue. Fine dust in the Martian air scatters light the opposite way Earth's sky does.
Apollo astronauts reported that fresh moon dust smells like spent gunpowder.
Phobos orbits Mars faster than the planet spins, so from the surface it rises in the west and sets in the east.
Despite being closest to the Sun, Mercury hides water ice in permanently shadowed craters at its poles.
Venus, not Mercury, is the hottest planet. Its thick carbon dioxide atmosphere traps heat at around 465 degrees Celsius.
It rains sulfuric acid on Venus, but the drops evaporate in the heat before they ever reach the ground.
Winds on Neptune can top 2,000 kilometers per hour, the fastest measured on any planet in the solar system.
Uranus rotates on its side, tilted about 98 degrees, likely knocked over by a massive collision long ago.
Opportunity, the Mars rover designed for a 90-day mission, kept driving for nearly 15 years.
Deep inside Uranus and Neptune, scientists think intense pressure squeezes carbon into showers of diamond rain.
The first planets ever found outside our solar system orbit a dead star, a pulsar, discovered in 1992.
Astronomers have confirmed more than 5,000 planets orbiting other stars, and the count keeps climbing.
Proxima Centauri, the nearest star beyond the Sun, is 4.2 light years away. A probe at Voyager speed would need over 70,000 years to reach it.
Betelgeuse is so enormous that if it replaced our Sun, its surface would swallow Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars.
In about 5 billion years, the Sun will balloon into a red giant, likely swallowing Mercury and Venus.
A white dwarf packs roughly the mass of the Sun into a ball about the size of Earth.
Light from the Andromeda galaxy takes 2.5 million years to reach us, yet you can spot it with the naked eye on a dark night.
Space is closer than you think. It officially begins about 100 kilometers up, an hour's drive if you could drive straight up.
In total, 24 humans have flown to the Moon and just 12 have walked on it. No one has been back since 1972.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space in 1961, completing one orbit of Earth in 108 minutes.
Valentina Tereshkova became the first woman in space in 1963, orbiting Earth 48 times over three days.
The computer that guided Apollo 11 to the Moon had far less processing power than a modern smartphone.
Australia is wider than the Moon. The Moon's diameter is about 3,500 kilometers, while Australia spans nearly 4,000.
On average, all seven other planets could line up side by side in the gap between Earth and the Moon.
A photon born in the Sun's core can take tens of thousands of years to escape to the surface, then just 8 minutes to reach Earth.
Every second, the Sun converts about 4 million tons of matter into pure energy.
Jupiter's magnetic bubble is the largest structure in the solar system. If it glowed, it would look bigger than the full Moon in our sky.
Io, a moon of Jupiter, is the most volcanically active world we know of, with hundreds of erupting volcanoes.
Saturn's rings span hundreds of thousands of kilometers yet are astonishingly thin, in places only about 10 meters thick.
Saturn's moon Enceladus shoots geysers of water into space from an ocean hidden beneath its ice.
A comet's tail always points away from the Sun, pushed by sunlight and solar wind, even when the comet is flying outward.
Halley's comet swings past Earth roughly every 76 years. Its next visit is due in 2061.
In 2017, astronomers spotted Oumuamua, the first known object to visit our solar system from another star.
All the asteroids in the asteroid belt combined add up to less mass than Earth's Moon.
Pluto is smaller than Earth's Moon, and it has not completed a single orbit of the Sun since its discovery in 1930.
New Horizons spent nine and a half years racing to Pluto, then flew past it in a matter of hours.
Parker Solar Probe is the fastest thing humans have ever built, reaching about 690,000 kilometers per hour.
In 2021, Parker Solar Probe flew through the Sun's outer atmosphere, becoming the first spacecraft to touch the Sun.
Hubble launched in 1990 with a flawed mirror. Astronauts flew up and repaired it in orbit, and it is still working today.
The James Webb Space Telescope orbits 1.5 million kilometers from Earth, four times farther away than the Moon.
Webb's giant mirrors are coated in gold, yet all of that gold together weighs about as much as a golf ball.
One light year is about 9.5 trillion kilometers. Even the fastest spacecraft ever built would need over a thousand years to cross one.
Shrink the Sun to the size of a grapefruit, and the nearest star would be another grapefruit nearly 3,000 kilometers away.
A single sugar cube of neutron star matter would weigh hundreds of millions of tons, roughly as much as all of humanity combined.
Time runs slower near a black hole. An astronaut hovering close to one would age less than everyone back home.
In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope captured the first ever photograph of a black hole, in the galaxy M87.
Black holes do not vacuum things up. If the Sun became a black hole of equal mass, Earth would keep orbiting normally.
KELT-9b is a planet hotter than many stars. Its dayside roasts at about 4,300 degrees Celsius.
TRAPPIST-1, a star 40 light years away, hosts seven roughly Earth-sized planets, several in the habitable zone.
Billions of rogue planets may drift through the Milky Way alone, orphan worlds with no star to orbit.
Our Milky Way galaxy contains somewhere between 100 billion and 400 billion stars. We genuinely do not know the exact number.
Astronomers estimate the observable universe contains hundreds of billions of galaxies, each with billions of stars.
About 95 percent of the universe is dark matter and dark energy. Everything we can actually see makes up just 5 percent.
Our solar system orbits the center of the Milky Way about once every 230 million years. Last lap, the dinosaurs were just getting started.
Right now you are moving about 30 kilometers per second as Earth races along its orbit around the Sun.
Astronauts say space has a smell. Suits and gear brought in from spacewalks smell like seared steak and hot metal.
In the vacuum of space, two clean pieces of bare metal that touch can fuse together permanently. It is called cold welding.
Space is silent. With no air to carry sound waves, even the biggest explosion happens without a whisper.
Exposed to the vacuum of space, the water in your body would start to boil at ordinary body temperature.
Cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov spent 437 straight days in orbit, the longest single spaceflight in history.
NASA compared astronaut Scott Kelly, after nearly a year in space, with his identical twin on Earth to study how spaceflight changes the body.
The International Space Station is about the size of a football field and is often visible from your backyard with the naked eye.
Humans have lived aboard the International Space Station continuously since November 2000, without a single day's break.
Food tastes bland in orbit because fluid shifts to astronauts' heads and dulls their sense of smell. Hot sauce is prized on the station.
The Moon has quakes. Moonquakes can rumble on for tens of minutes because the Moon vibrates like a struck bell.
A single solar day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days, twice as long as Mercury's entire year.
Helium was discovered in the Sun's light in 1868, decades before anyone found it on Earth. Its name comes from Helios, the Sun.
Neptune was found with math. Astronomers predicted its position from wobbles in Uranus's orbit before ever seeing it in 1846.
Uranus was the first planet discovered with a telescope, spotted by William Herschel in 1781.
The dwarf planet Eris is more massive than Pluto. Its discovery is what got Pluto demoted in 2006.
Apollo astronauts left mirrors on the Moon, and scientists still bounce lasers off them to measure its distance to the millimeter.
GPS satellites must correct for Einstein's relativity. Without it, your map position would drift by kilometers every day.
Our Sun is actually white. It only looks yellow because Earth's atmosphere scatters away the blue light.
Much of the gold in your jewelry was likely forged in collisions between neutron stars, long before Earth existed.
Nearly every element in your body heavier than hydrogen was made inside a star. You are literally built from stardust.
Water recycling on the space station is so good that about 98 percent of the crew's water, including urine and sweat, gets reused.
A giant dust cloud near the center of our galaxy contains ethyl formate, the same chemical that gives raspberries their flavor.
Ingenuity, a small helicopter, made the first powered flight on another planet in 2021, carrying a scrap of the Wright brothers' first plane.
Sunspots look dark only by contrast. Cut one out and hung in the night sky, it would glow brighter than the full Moon.
Saturn has a bizarre hexagon-shaped jet stream swirling at its north pole, wide enough to fit two Earths inside.
Hundreds of millions of years ago, Earth's day lasted only about 22 hours. The Moon's pull keeps slowing our spin.
Apollo spacesuits were sewn by hand at a division of Playtex, the company famous for making bras.
Total solar eclipses are a cosmic coincidence. The Sun is about 400 times wider than the Moon, but also about 400 times farther away.
Earth is actually farthest from the Sun in early July. Seasons come from our planet's tilt, not its distance.
The Boomerang Nebula is the coldest known natural place, about 1 degree above absolute zero, colder than deep space itself.
